coredata.dc:title: Effect of graphene quantum dots and curing ways on properties of serpentine mortar
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Jianjun
Tian, Geng
Zhang, Zhiheng
Zou, Pinyu
Li, Hongle
Wang, Yihan
Zhang, Jie
coredata.dc:description: To enhance the mechanical and radiation shielding properties of serpentine mortar, this study systematically investigated the effects of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the flexural strength, compressive strength and crystal water loss rate of serpentine mortar. The mechanical properties and microstructure of specimens under different curing regimes were tested by macroscopic mechanical tests combined with X-ray diffraction (XRD) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The incorporation of 0.03 wt% GQDs significantly enhanced the performance of the composite material. Test results indicate that GQDs effectively reduce the crystal water loss rate at elevated temperatures. Specifically, at 500 °C, the loss rate decreased from 4.77 % to 4.14 %, representing a reduction of 13.2 %. Furthermore, the compressive strength of the BY-0.03 % group exhibited a 12 % improvement compared to the control group. Under the combined effects of carbonization curing and GQDs, the CY-0.03 % group achieved a compressive strength of 21.36 MPa within seven days. Remarkably, the GY-0.03 %-80 group attained a compressive strength of 17.1 MPa within just three days.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanomaterials
Radiation shielding
Graphene quantum dots
Curing period

The authors thank Guangdong Shunzhuo Energy Technology Co. Ltd for providing the graphene quantum dots. The author also thanks Shaanxi Tianbao Mining for providing serpentine aggregate.

The authors thank Guangdong Shunzhuo Energy Technology Co., Ltd for providing the graphene quantum dots. The author also thanks Shaanxi Tianbao Mining for providing serpentine aggregate.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM of GQDs; (b) Serpentine morphology; (c) FTIR of GQDs; (d)XRD of Serpentine.

(a) Mechanical properties of serpentine mortar under various curing methods; (b) XRD of serpentine mortar under various curing methods; (c) High temperature Muffle furnace; (d) Crystal water loss rate of specimens with different GQDs content.
(a) Control group; (b) BY-0.03% group; (c) GY-0.03%-80 group; (d) CY-0.03% group.

row.entry: After carbonization curing for 4d, continue standard curing until 7d

head.ce:title: Effect of graphene quantum dots and curing ways on properties of serpentine mortar

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Civil Engineering, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Civil Engineering, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To enhance the mechanical and radiation shielding properties of serpentine mortar, this study systematically investigated the effects of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the flexural strength, compressive strength and crystal water loss rate of serpentine mortar. The mechanical properties and microstructure of specimens under different curing regimes were tested by macroscopic mechanical tests combined with X-ray diffraction (XRD) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The incorporation of 0.03 wt% GQDs significantly enhanced the performance of the composite material. Test results indicate that GQDs effectively reduce the crystal water loss rate at elevated temperatures. Specifically, at 500 °C, the loss rate decreased from 4.77 % to 4.14 %, representing a reduction of 13.2 %. Furthermore, the compressive strength of the BY-0.03 % group exhibited a 12 % improvement compared to the control group. Under the combined effects of carbonization curing and GQDs, the CY-0.03 % group achieved a compressive strength of 21.36 MPa within seven days. Remarkably, the GY-0.03 %-80 group attained a compressive strength of 17.1 MPa within just three days.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Traditional radiation-shielding concrete typically has an apparent density exceeding 3000 kg/m3, resulting in considerable weight and posing substantial challenges for the transportation and storage of spent nuclear fuel [1]. Consequently, there is an urgent need to develop lightweight, high-strength storage containers for spent nuclear fuel. Serpentine minerals, characterized by low density, high crystalline water content, and excellent heat resistance [2], are increasingly used in neutron shielding [3]. Serpentine tailings demonstrate significant potential for applications in lightweight spent fuel storage drums and as a material for repairing structural cracks in nuclear power facilities (see Fig. 1 ).

Research indicates that serpentine in cement-based materials suffers from low strength [4], limiting its use to non-load-bearing components. Adding nanomaterials as reinforcements can significantly improve the mechanical properties of cement. Win et al. [5] reported that graphene quantum dots (GQDs) improve cement mortar's compressive and flexural strength by 40 % and 108 %, while significantly reducing micropores. Currently, no studies have been reported on the application of GQDs in serpentine-based mortars, and research combining GQDs with specialized curing methods remains extremely limited.

Serpentine often contains high levels of asbestos [6]. Asbestos particles smaller than 1.18 mm are visually indistinguishable from serpentine particles. In this study, sand sized between 1.18 mm and 4.75 mm was used as the aggregate. The GQDs solution was prepared by stirring for 30 min, followed by ultrasonic dispersion for another 30 min, using 250 g of tap water and 0, 0.15, or 0.3 g of GQDs. The materials included P.O 42.5-grade cement (350 g), slag (100 g), fly ash (50 g), water-reducing agent (5 g), serpentine sand (1.18–2.36 mm, 600 g), and serpentine sand (2.36–4.75 mm, 500 g). Specimen dimensions were 40 × 40 × 160 mm. The serpentine mortar mix ratio was determined based on GB/T 17671–2021, “Test method of cement mortar strength (ISO method).” Each group consisted of three specimens, and the average value of the three measurements was calculated in accordance with the specifications.

Exothermic nuclear reactions in power plants generate heat, and crystallized water loss indirectly reflects neutron radiation shielding capacity [7]. The experimental procedure involved curing specimens containing GQDs for 28 days. Specimens were oven-dried at 105 °C for 48 h to remove free water and stabilize their weight. The masses of the specimens were recorded before and after high-temperature exposure to assess crystallized water loss. The dried specimens were heated from 105 °C to 300 °C or 500 °C at a rate of 4 °C/min. Upon reaching the target temperature, the specimens were held isothermally for 2 h. Subsequently, the specimens were cooled to 105 °C, removed from the oven, allowed to cool to room temperature, and weighed. Crystallized water loss was calculated using Eq. (1).
(1)
a =
m 0
-
m T
m 0
where
a
is the rate of crystal water loss;
m 0
is the mass of the cement mortar specimen at 105 °C,
m T
is the mass of cement mortar specimen at 300 °C and 500 °C.
A small serpentine mortar core, less than 1 cm in size, was soaked in anhydrous ethanol for 7 days, vacuum freeze-dried, gold-coated, and then scanned.

Fig. 2 (a) illustrates the effects of curing methods and GQDs on the flexural and compressive strengths of serpentine mortar. After 28 days, the control group’s flexural and compressive strengths were 5.84 MPa and 21.7 MPa. At 0.03 wt% GQDs, these values rose to 6.01 MPa and 24.3 MPa, improving by 2.91 % and 12 %. However, At 0.06 wt% GQDs, flexural and compressive strengths dropped to 5.28 MPa and 17.7 MPa, decreasing by 9.6 % and 18.4 %. Moderate GQDs concentrations improve mechanical properties, while excessive amounts degrade performance [8]. This degradation results from GQDs agglomeration at high concentrations [9].

Curing at 80 °C for 2 days yielded 17.1 MPa compressive strength, showing that elevated temperatures enhance early strength. Fig. 2(b) shows higher curing temperatures accelerate hydration, increasing hydration product peaks compared to the control. Carbonation curing further enhanced the mechanical properties, with compressive strength reaching 21.36 MPa. This method not only improves early strength but also reduces curing time and accelerates construction schedules. As shown in Fig. 2(b), carbonation curing transforms CH, C-S-H, C3S, and unhydrated CaO into CaCO3.

Fig. 2(d) reveals that incorporating GQDs significantly reduces the crystallized water loss in serpentine mortar. At 300 °C, the crystallized water loss rates for specimens with 0 %, 0.03 wt%, and 0.06 wt% GQDs were 2.6 %, 2.23 %, and 2.34 %, respectively, corresponding to reductions of 14.2 % and 10.0 % for 0.03 wt% and 0.06 wt% GQDs compared to the control. At 500 °C, these rates were 4.77 %, 4.14 %, and 4.25 %, representing decreases of 13.2 % and 10.9 %, respectively. This reduction is attributed to the excellent thermal conductivity of uniformly dispersed GQDs [10], which ensures homogeneous internal heating and minimizes localized thermal stress at elevated temperatures. The crystallization water in serpentine mortar specimens is primarily stored within the serpentine aggregates. When the cement paste surrounding the aggregates contains uniformly distributed GQDs, the evaporation of crystallization water from the aggregate surface at high temperatures forms hydrogen bonds with the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups in the GQDs. These hydrogen bonds, with higher bond energy than van der Waals forces, more effectively restrain crystallization water, thereby reducing its loss in serpentine.

Fig. 3 (a) shows that CH in the control group accumulates excessively, hindering secondary hydration. Fig. 3(b) shows that GQDs enhance microstructure by promoting C-S-H and AFt formation, which interweave to improve mechanical properties. Fig. 3(c) indicates that during dry heat curing, higher temperatures reduce CH and AFt crystals while forming abundant C-S-H gel. This leads to rapid early strength in the GY-0.03 % group, but prolonged high temperatures cause cracks in the cement stone. Fig. 3(d) shows that CO2 ingress converts hexagonal CH crystals into calcite. GQDs act as nucleation sites, accelerating C-S-H gel and AFt crystal growth. Compared to standard curing, carbonation curing forms dense AFt and C-S-H intergrowth, filling pores and enhancing structural density.

The addition of 0.03 wt% GQDs to serpentine mortar has three primary effects: (1) at 500°C, the crystallization water loss decreases from 4.77 % to 4.14 %, representing a reduction of 13.2 %; (2) the compressive strength of the specimens increases by 12 %; (3) the internal microstructure becomes denser. Under the synergistic effect of GQDs and two curing methods, the CY-0.03 % group achieved a compressive strength of 21.36 MPa within 7 days, while the GY-0.03 %-80 group reached 17.1 MPa in just 3 days.

Jianjun Shi: Resources, Project administration. Geng Tian: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhiheng Zhang: Project administration. Pinyu Zou: Supervision, Investigation. Hongle Li: Project administration, Investigation. Yihan Wang: Methodology. Jie Zhang: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support received from Nature Science Foundation of Hunan Province (Project No. 14JJ2083), Graduate Research Innovation Project of University of South China (Grant No. 243YXC022). The authors thank Guangdong Shunzhuo Energy Technology Co., Ltd for providing the graphene quantum dots. The author also thanks Shaanxi Tianbao Mining for providing serpentine aggregate.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A comprehensive review of radiation shielding concrete: Properties, design, evaluation, and applications
Struct. Concr.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Barbhuiya, B.B. Das, P. Norman, T. Qureshi, A comprehensive review of radiation shielding concrete: Properties, design, evaluation, and applications, Structural Concrete (2024).

Constr. Build. Mater.
